input: Apple Inc. reported Q3 FY2024 results: revenue of $85.8B (up 5% YoY), net income $21.4B, EPS $1.40 vs $1.26 expected. iPhone revenue $39.3B (-1% YoY), Services revenue $24.2B (+14% YoY), record for Services. Gross margin 46.3% vs 44.5% prior year. Cash and equivalents $153B. Guidance for Q4: revenue $85-88B. CEO Tim Cook highlighted AI integration into iOS 18 and expanding India manufacturing. The stock rose 3.2% after-hours.
expected: BULLISH. Services hitting record $24.2B (+14% YoY) with expanding 46.3% gross margins signals a structural shift from hardware dependency. $153B cash reserve + AI/iOS18 catalyst and India manufacturing diversification reduce risk. Q4 guidance midpoint ($86.5B) implies continued momentum. Key risk: iPhone decline (-1%) must stabilize. Accumulate on dips, 12-month target $210-230.

input: Tesla Q2 2024: Revenue $25.2B (+2% YoY), automotive revenue $19.8B (-7% YoY), energy generation $3.0B (+100% YoY). Net income $1.48B (-45% YoY), automotive gross margin 18.5% vs 18.1% prior quarter. Deliveries 443,956 units (-5% YoY). Free cash flow $1.34B. Price cuts totaling 25% over 18 months. Elon Musk reaffirmed Robotaxi event October 2024 and 20-30% vehicle volume growth in 2025.
expected: CAUTIOUS. Automotive margin recovery to 18.5% is encouraging but 25% cumulative price cuts have eroded brand premium with no floor visible. Energy doubling is real but too small ($3B) to offset core weakness. Delivery decline (-5% YoY) undermines the growth thesis. Robotaxi and 2025 volume promises are speculative. Avoid adding; watch Q3 delivery data as make-or-break signal.

input: Nvidia Q1 FY2025: Revenue $26.0B (+262% YoY, +18% QoQ), Data Center $22.6B (+427% YoY). Net income $14.9B, EPS $5.98 vs $5.59 expected. Gross margin 78.4%. Q2 guidance: $28B ±2%. Blackwell GPU architecture shipments beginning Q2. Hyperscaler customers all increasing AI capex. $10B buyback authorized.
expected: STRONG BUY. 78.4% gross margin at $26B scale is exceptional. Data center at $22.6B reflects structural hyperscaler AI capex commitments, not a one-quarter anomaly. $28B Q2 guidance (+7.7% QoQ) shows no demand cliff. Blackwell transition risk is manageable given continued H100 demand. Add on any >10% pullback; only risks are geopolitical export controls and a black-swan capex pullback.

input: Bed Bath & Beyond filed Chapter 11 bankruptcy January 2023. Last reported quarter: revenue $1.26B (-33% YoY), same-store sales -32%, gross margin 16% from 38% two years prior. Inventory write-downs $100M+. $1.82B total debt vs $100M cash. Lost 4 of 5 C-suite executives in 6 months. 150 store closures announced. Vendors requiring cash-on-delivery. DIP financing $240M secured.
expected: AVOID. Gross margin collapse (38% → 16%) signals vendor breakdown and desperate discounting simultaneously. $1.82B debt vs $100M cash with COD requirements means liquidity depends entirely on DIP financing. -32% same-store sales has no floor. Executive exodus removes execution optionality. Equity is likely worthless; any rally is a short opportunity. Do not hold any position.

input: Microsoft Q3 FY2024: Revenue $61.9B (+17% YoY), Azure +21% YoY, Office 365 commercial +15%. Operating income $27.6B, margin 44.6%. EPS $2.94 vs $2.82 expected. AI Copilot: 1.8M paying seats. Capex $14B (+79% YoY). Free cash flow $20.3B. Q4 guidance: $63.5-64.5B. Azure growth expected to accelerate in H2.
expected: BULLISH. 44.6% operating margin at $61.9B scale with accelerating growth is rare. Azure +21% with guided acceleration demonstrates durable cloud share gains. 1.8M Copilot seats at $30/user/month = $650M+ ARR growing rapidly. $20.3B FCF funds buybacks comfortably. Risk: $14B capex cycle must convert to revenue — watch Azure for deceleration. Strong buy on any weakness.

input: Meta Platforms Q1 2024: Revenue $36.5B (+27% YoY), net income $12.4B (+117% YoY), EPS $4.71 vs $4.32 expected. Operating margin 38% vs 25% prior year. Daily active users 3.24B. Reality Labs loss $3.85B. 2024 capex guidance raised to $35-40B. AI recommendation engine driving +7% time-on-platform. Q2 guidance revenue $36.5-39B.
expected: BULLISH. Operating margin expansion from 25% to 38% on $36.5B revenue is the headline — this is the 'Year of Efficiency' payoff in full. AI-driven +7% time-on-platform directly monetizes as ad revenue, and 3.24B DAU gives unmatched scale. Reality Labs burning $3.85B is the chronic drag but manageable against $12.4B net income. Raised capex ($35-40B) is a bet on AI infrastructure that should compound. Hold core; add on any regulatory-driven dip.

input: Amazon Q1 2024: Revenue $143.3B (+13% YoY), AWS $25B (+17% YoY), advertising $11.8B (+24% YoY). Operating income $15.3B vs $4.8B prior year (+219%). EPS $0.98 vs $0.83 expected. Free cash flow $50.1B trailing twelve months. North America retail margin 5.8% vs 1.2% prior year. Q2 guidance operating income $10-14B.
expected: BULLISH. The retail margin recovery (1.2% → 5.8%) proves the logistics cost-cutting thesis — Amazon is extracting profitability from a business once written off as a margin trap. AWS re-acceleration to +17% YoY plus advertising at +24% means two high-margin businesses are compounding simultaneously. $50.1B trailing FCF is a war chest. Q2 operating income guidance range ($10-14B) is wide but the midpoint ($12B) maintains momentum. Core holding; add on market-wide pullbacks.

input: Peloton Q3 FY2024: Revenue $717.7M (-4% YoY), connected fitness subscriptions 2.97M (-1% QoQ), churn rate 1.8%. Net loss $167.1M, operating cash flow -$6.4M. Hardware revenue $165M (-19% YoY). Cost cuts: $200M annualized savings achieved. New CEO Barry McCarthy resigned, interim leadership in place. Debt $1.7B, cash $748M. Partnership with Lululemon announced.
expected: AVOID. Subscription churn at 1.8% monthly on a declining base (2.97M, -1% QoQ) is a slow bleed — the core metric is moving the wrong direction with no reversal catalyst. Hardware -19% YoY kills any hardware-led recovery thesis. $1.7B debt vs $748M cash with negative operating cash flow (-$6.4M) leaves no margin for error. CEO departure mid-turnaround destroys execution confidence. Cost cuts help but don't address demand erosion. Lululemon partnership is marketing, not a business model fix. Do not own.

input: JPMorgan Chase Q1 2024: Revenue $42.5B (+8% YoY), net income $13.4B (+6% YoY), EPS $4.44 vs $4.11 expected. Net interest income $23.2B (+11% YoY). ROE 17%, ROTCE 21%. CET1 ratio 15.0% (well above 12.3% regulatory minimum). Provision for credit losses $1.88B (higher than expected). Charge-off rate 0.54%. CEO Jamie Dimon warned of 'significant risks ahead' including geopolitics and inflation persistence.
expected: BULLISH / CORE HOLDING. 21% ROTCE at scale is best-in-class among megabanks and the 15.0% CET1 ratio (vs 12.3% requirement) gives 270bps of excess capital for buybacks or M&A optionality. NII +11% YoY shows the rate environment is still a tailwind. Charge-off rate of 0.54% is benign despite higher provisions — management is being prudent, not signaling deterioration. Dimon's macro warnings are his standard communication style and not balance-sheet specific. Hold; buy on any credit-fear driven selloff below 1.8x tangible book.

input: Moderna Q1 2024: Revenue $167M (-91% YoY), net loss $1.18B. COVID vaccine sales collapsed as expected. R&D spend $1.1B. Pipeline: 48 programs, 6 in Phase 3 (RSV, flu, melanoma combo, CMV, rare disease). Cash $12.2B. Guidance: 2024 revenue $4B from COVID sales. RSV vaccine FDA decision expected Q3 2024. Burn rate ~$1B/quarter.
expected: NEUTRAL / SPECULATIVE. The COVID revenue collapse (-91%) is priced in — the real question is pipeline conversion. $12.2B cash at $1B/quarter burn buys 12 quarters of runway, which is enough for multiple Phase 3 readouts. The RSV FDA decision is a near-term binary catalyst. Melanoma combination therapy (with Merck) is the high-conviction moonshot. This is a pipeline optionality play, not a revenue story. Only buy if you have a view on RSV approval probability; otherwise wait for Phase 3 data before sizing up.

input: Shopify Q1 2024: Revenue $1.86B (+23% YoY), gross merchandise volume $60.9B (+23% YoY), gross profit $956M (+33% YoY). Operating income $86M vs -$193M prior year. Free cash flow $232M ($86M prior year). Merchant solutions revenue $1.41B (+20% YoY). Subscription revenue $511M (+34% YoY). Sold logistics business for $2.1B. Headcount down 20% YoY.
expected: BULLISH. The pivot is working: divesting logistics and cutting 20% of headcount unlocked $232M FCF vs. negative prior year — this is a quality of earnings inflection, not a one-quarter anomaly. Gross profit +33% outpacing revenue +23% means mix shift toward higher-margin subscription (+34%) is accelerating. GMV at $60.9B (+23%) confirms merchant retention. The logistics divestiture removes the capex drag that suppressed margins for two years. Buy; target $80-95 on continued FCF expansion.

input: Disney Q2 FY2024: Revenue $22.1B (+1% YoY), net income $216M vs $1.27B prior year. Direct-to-consumer (streaming) segment profit $47M first ever quarterly profit vs -$659M prior year. Parks revenue $8.4B (+10% YoY), margin 26%. Linear TV revenue declining -7% YoY. Total streaming subscribers 153M. Capex $1.3B. CEO Bob Iger announced $1B cost cuts completed, $7.5B buyback authorized.
expected: BULLISH / TURNAROUND. Streaming reaching first-ever profitability ($47M vs -$659M YoY) is the inflection the thesis has been waiting for — the bleeding has stopped. Parks at $8.4B (+10%) with 26% margin remains a cash engine. The $7.5B buyback signals confidence in FCF generation post-restructuring. The real risk is linear TV -7% YoY accelerating — that's a structural decline with no floor. Net income collapse ($1.27B → $216M) reflects transition costs, not steady-state. Buy; but sizing should be moderate until streaming reaches $500M+ quarterly profit run-rate.

input: Rivian Q1 2024: Revenue $1.2B, production 13,980 units, deliveries 13,588 units. Gross profit -$527M (-$45,923 per vehicle). Net loss $1.48B. Cash $7.9B. 2024 production guidance maintained at 57,000 units. Gross profit per unit improving QoQ by ~$6,000. Volkswagen investment deal $5B announced post-quarter. Operating cash flow -$1.5B.
expected: CAUTIOUS. Losing $45,923 per vehicle on gross margin alone with $1.5B operating cash burn quarterly is existential math — the VW deal ($5B) buys roughly 3 quarters of runway at current burn. The $6,000/unit improvement QoQ is the only positive signal, but at 57,000 units annual guidance the path to gross profit breakeven is 2025 at earliest under optimistic assumptions. VW partnership validates technology but doesn't fix unit economics today. Speculative hold for believers in the VW partnership; new money should wait for gross profit breakeven before entering.

input: Eli Lilly Q1 2024: Revenue $8.77B (+26% YoY), Mounjaro (tirzepatide) $1.8B, Zepbound (obesity) $517M first full quarter. EPS $2.58 vs $2.46 expected. Raised 2024 guidance to $42.4-43.6B (prior $40.4-41.6B). Gross margin 80.5%. Manufacturing capacity expansion: $9B capex committed over 3 years. GLP-1 market estimated $150B+ by 2030. Supply constrained.
expected: STRONG BUY. Mounjaro + Zepbound combined $2.3B in a single quarter on a supply-constrained basis — when manufacturing capacity comes online ($9B capex over 3 years), revenue could 3-4x from current levels. 80.5% gross margin means every incremental dollar of capacity expansion is highly accretive. Guidance raise of $2B midpoint in a single quarter signals demand visibility. The GLP-1 market at $150B+ is a multi-year structural tailwind. Only risk is competitive response from Novo Nordisk. Core long-term holding; add aggressively on any pullback.

input: WeWork filed Chapter 11 bankruptcy November 2023. Last reported quarter pre-bankruptcy: Revenue $844M, occupancy rate 72% (down from 80%). Net loss $397M. Total debt $18.65B. Monthly cash burn $200M+. Lease liabilities $13B. Attempted to renegotiate 400+ leases. Softbank total investment $16.5B, current equity value near zero. Co-working market recovering post-COVID but WeWork failed to capitalize.
expected: AVOID / WORTHLESS. $18.65B debt against $844M quarterly revenue at 72% occupancy — even at 100% occupancy the unit economics don't support the debt load. $200M+ monthly cash burn with $13B in lease liabilities means bankruptcy was structural, not cyclical. The co-working market recovering while WeWork deteriorated proves this is company-specific failure, not industry headwinds. Equity is zero. Bonds trading at distressed levels reflect recovery uncertainty. No position; this is a case study, not an investment.

input: Palantir Q1 2024: Revenue $634M (+21% YoY), US commercial revenue $150M (+40% YoY), US government $257M (+12% YoY). Net income $106M (GAAP profitable for 5th consecutive quarter). Rule of 40 score: 57. Customer count +42% YoY to 262 US commercial customers. AIP (AI Platform) bootcamp driving accelerated sales cycles from 6 months to 2-3 weeks. EPS $0.04.
expected: BULLISH. AIP-driven sales cycle compression (6 months → 2-3 weeks) is a leading indicator that hasn't fully shown up in revenue yet — US commercial at +40% YoY is accelerating, and 262 customers growing 42% YoY suggests the top of the funnel is expanding. Fifth consecutive GAAP profitability quarter at a Rule of 40 score of 57 puts this in elite software company territory. Government at +12% is slower but predictable. Key risk: $634M revenue base means absolute dollars still modest, and the stock often trades at 25-30x revenue — valuation is the bear case. Hold; buy on any 20%+ drawdown.

input: Block (Square) Q1 2024: Revenue $5.96B (+19% YoY), gross profit $2.09B (+22% YoY), net loss $40M. Cash App gross profit $1.26B (+25% YoY), Cash App monthly actives 57M (+7% YoY). Square gross profit $820M (+19% YoY). Bitcoin revenue $2.45B (+75% YoY, minimal margin). Adjusted EBITDA $705M. CEO Jack Dorsey announced AI and hardware investments accelerating.
expected: BULLISH. Strip out Bitcoin pass-through and the real business — Cash App + Square — generated $2.09B gross profit (+22% YoY). Cash App at $1.26B (+25%) with 57M MAUs deepening financial services penetration is the growth engine. Square +19% shows SMB resilience. $705M adjusted EBITDA is real cash generation. The GAAP net loss is noise from stock comp and Bitcoin volatility. Risk: Jack Dorsey's attention split between Block and X/Twitter remains a governance concern. Buy on dips; target $90-110 on gross profit multiple expansion.

input: Exxon Mobil Q1 2024: Revenue $83.1B (+3% YoY), net income $8.2B (-28% YoY), EPS $2.06 vs $2.20 expected. Production 4.2M barrels/day (+2% YoY). Capital expenditure $6.8B. Pioneer Natural Resources acquisition completed: $60B deal adds Permian Basin assets. Free cash flow $10.1B. Dividend $0.95/share (+4% YoY). Share buyback $5B/quarter pace. Brent crude assumed $65/barrel for planning purposes.
expected: BULLISH / INCOME. Net income declining -28% YoY is entirely oil price driven — production at 4.2M bpd (+2%) and the Pioneer integration adding Permian scale are execution positives. $10.1B FCF at $65/barrel planning assumption means the dividend ($0.95/share, 4% yield) and $5B/quarter buyback are well covered even in a downcycle. Pioneer closes the Permian gap with Chevron. Risk: oil price is the only variable that matters here, and a move to $55/barrel compresses FCF materially. Hold for income; add if Brent pulls back to $70 as an entry.

input: Airbnb Q1 2024: Revenue $2.14B (+18% YoY), net income $264M vs -$19M prior year. Nights and experiences booked 133M (+9% YoY). Average daily rate $174 (+4% YoY). Free cash flow $1.9B trailing twelve months. Gross booking value $22.9B (+12% YoY). Active listings 7.7M (+15% YoY). Guidance Q2 revenue $2.68-2.74B (+8-11% YoY). No long-term debt.
expected: BULLISH. $1.9B trailing FCF with zero long-term debt on a $2.14B quarterly revenue run rate is a pristine balance sheet. ADR holding at $174 (+4%) despite 15% more supply (7.7M listings) proves pricing power and demand elasticity. The -$19M → +$264M net income swing in one year reflects genuine operating leverage, not accounting adjustments. Q2 guidance (+8-11%) is conservative given their track record of beating. Risk: regulatory risk in key cities (NYC, Barcelona) could remove meaningful supply. Core hold; buy below 25x FCF.

input: Kohl's Q4 2023: Revenue $5.67B (-4.3% YoY), comparable sales -4.3%, gross margin 32.4% (-80bps YoY). Net income $186M. Inventory $3.2B (-8% YoY) — positive. Full-year 2024 guidance: comparable sales -2% to +1%, EPS $1.85-2.25. Sephora shop-in-shop now in 910 stores, contributing high-single-digit sales growth where present. Dividend $0.50/share/quarter (yield ~7%). $500M debt due 2024.
expected: CAUTIOUS / NEUTRAL. Comparable sales -4.3% with guidance for continued softness (-2% to +1%) signals Kohl's hasn't solved its traffic problem. Gross margin compression (-80bps) with negative comps is a difficult combination. The Sephora partnership is the one genuine bright spot — high-single-digit comp lift in those stores proves the concept, but rolling out to all stores takes time and capital. 7% dividend yield is attractive but sustainable only if EPS ($1.85-2.25 guided) holds — $500M debt due in 2024 is manageable but adds pressure. Hold for income investors only; not a growth position.

input: Snowflake Q4 FY2024: Product revenue $738.1M (+33% YoY), net revenue retention 131%. Remaining performance obligations $5.2B (+41% YoY). Operating loss -$335M, adjusted operating margin 6%. Net loss -$886M GAAP. CEO Frank Slootman retired, replaced by Sridhar Ramaswamy (former Google AI lead). Q1 FY2025 guidance: product revenue $745-750M (+26% YoY, deceleration from 33%).
expected: CAUTIOUS. The guidance deceleration from 33% to 26% YoY growth, combined with CEO departure mid-AI-cycle, is a double overhang. 131% NRR is excellent and $5.2B RPO (+41%) confirms enterprise stickiness, but the market is paying 20x+ revenue for a growth story that is visibly slowing. Ramaswamy's AI credentials are relevant but untested in this role. Adjusted operating margin of 6% is thin for the valuation. Wait for Q1 execution under new CEO before adding; if NRR holds above 130% and growth re-accelerates, revisit. Trim on strength.

input: Ford Motor Q1 2024: Revenue $42.8B (+3% YoY), adjusted EBIT $2.8B. Ford Pro (commercial vehicles): revenue $13.3B, EBIT margin 15%. Ford Blue (ICE): EBIT margin 7.4%. Ford Model e (EVs): EBIT -$1.3B, loss per vehicle ~$100,000. EV production cut: 2024 target lowered from 200K to ~150K vehicles. Total EV losses 2023-2024 expected $5.5B. Dividend reinstated $0.15/share.
expected: NEUTRAL. Ford Pro at 15% EBIT margin is a genuine standout — commercial vehicles are underappreciated and this segment alone justifies holding the stock. But $100,000 loss per EV with $5.5B cumulative EV losses and a production cut is an admission that the EV strategy is broken at current scale. Ford Blue's 7.4% ICE margin is stable but structurally declining. The dividend reinstatement signals confidence in Ford Pro cash generation. Thesis: own it for Ford Pro, tolerate Model e drag. Neutral overall; buy only if you believe Ford Pro can offset EV losses indefinitely.

input: Zoom Video Q4 FY2024: Revenue $1.146B (+3% YoY), enterprise revenue $672M (+5% YoY), net income $371M GAAP. Operating cash flow $426M. Customers with $100K+ ARR: 3,225 (+10% YoY). Net dollar expansion rate 98% (below 100% — net contraction territory). Q1 FY2025 guidance: revenue $1.125-1.13B (flat YoY). Cash $7.5B, no debt.
expected: NEUTRAL / VALUE TRAP WATCH. Net dollar expansion below 100% (at 98%) means the installed base is shrinking in revenue terms — that's a structural signal, not a one-quarter blip. Guidance for flat revenue YoY confirms this is a business in managed decline, not stabilization. $371M GAAP net income and $7.5B cash with no debt mean this is cheap on earnings, but cheap-for-a-reason. Enterprise customers growing +10% is the only growth signal, but too slowly to offset SMB churn. Hold only for deep value; no growth thesis remains. Avoid adding.

input: Costco Q2 FY2024: Revenue $58.4B (+6% YoY), comparable sales +5.6% (ex-FX, ex-fuel). Membership fee revenue $1.11B (+8% YoY), renewal rate 90.5% US/Canada. Net income $1.74B (+19% YoY), EPS $3.92 vs $3.62 expected. E-commerce +18% YoY. Gross margin 12.6% (deliberately thin). Membership fee increase expected (first since 2017). No debt, $14B cash.
expected: BULLISH / LONG-TERM CORE. 90.5% membership renewal rate at +8% fee revenue growth is a compounding annuity — Costco's moat is behavioral, not competitive. A membership fee increase (first since 2017) is a high-probability near-term earnings catalyst since nearly all fee revenue flows straight to operating income. E-commerce +18% shows the model is not structurally at risk from Amazon. 12.6% gross margins are intentionally thin — the genius is the fee model. Net income +19% on 6% revenue growth demonstrates operating leverage. Expensive on multiples, but deservedly so. Hold forever; add on 15%+ market-driven pullbacks.

input: SolarEdge Q1 2024: Revenue $204.4M (-78% YoY), gross margin -1.6% (negative). Net loss $201.7M. Inventory $1.2B (excess channel inventory issue). Headcount reduced 16%. European installer demand collapse post-subsidy changes. US residential solar market weak. Cash $674M, debt $695M. Management guided Q2 $200-230M revenue (flat). CEO departed Q4 2023.
expected: AVOID. Negative gross margin (-1.6%) means SolarEdge is losing money on every product sold before operating expenses — that is not a demand problem, that is a structural crisis. -78% revenue YoY with $1.2B in excess inventory and flat Q2 guidance signals the channel destocking has months to run. $674M cash vs $695M debt with $200M+ quarterly net losses means the runway is under 4 quarters without external financing. CEO departure accelerates execution risk. Even at distressed valuations, avoid until gross margin returns to positive territory — that is the minimum threshold for a recovery thesis.
